original_abstract:
This chapter examines the historical evolution of central–local government relations in Japan through the lenses of administration, finance, and politics. By utilizing a conceptual framework of "interfusion" versus "separation," the analysis clarifies the unique structural characteristics of Japanese governance. During its modernization in the Meiji era, Japan looked to continental European models and adopted an interfusion system, in which the central government delegates the implementation of national policies to local authorities rather than executing them through its own local branches. This relationship has proven remarkably resilient despite shifting political landscapes: it survived the functional centralization of the wartime regime, the institutional decentralization reforms introduced during the post-WWII Occupation, and the more recent wave of decentralization reforms starting in the 1990s. 

The study highlights how the interfusion model has been consistently reinforced through large-scale municipal mergers and the establishment of a robust fiscal adjustment system. While Japan stands out internationally for its high ratio of local government activity relative to total government spending, the chapter argues that this is fundamentally supported by the deeply integrated nature of the central–local relationship. Despite contemporary challenges such as demographic decline and crisis management, this interfused structure remains a defining pillar of the Japanese state.
humanized_abstract:
This chapter draws on the perspectives of administration, finance, and politics to describe the historical development of Japanese central–local relations. Using the bifurcation model of “interfusion” and “separation,” it structurally identifies the governing characteristics of Japan. At the time of Japan’s modernization in the Meiji period, the Japanese imitated the systems of contintental Europe and chose to introduce an interfusion system in which policy decisions are implemented by the local government agency rather than by an agent of the national government. The interfusion system has remained robust, despite occasional shifts in the direction of central–local relations, whether a functional centralization by the wartime regime, the structural decentralization enforced by the occupying powers during the post-war period, or the additional wave of reforms of the 1990s and beyond.

The interfusion model has been confirmed through large-scale municipal mergers and the creation of a strong system of fiscal constraint. Japan has one of the highest ratios of subnational public expenditure to total public sector expenditure in the world, but this is justified as being possible only in a highly integrated central-local system. The interfused structure of the Japanese state, which has provided a basis for the country’s politico-administrative system, has also survived the post-2000 challenges associated with the country’s declining population and its increasing ability to cope with large-scale crises.
